content:
# Genesis Chapter 12 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7r–7v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_012_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_012_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 8–9 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now the LORD said to Abram, "Go from your land, and from your kindred, and from your father's house, to the land that I will show you.

**2** And I will make of you a great nation, and I will bless you and make your name great, so that you will be a blessing.

**3** And I will bless those who bless you, and him who dishonors you I will curse, and in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed."

**4** So Abram went, as the LORD had told him, and Lot went with him. And Abram was seventy-five years old when he departed from Haran.

**5** And Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son, and all their possessions that they had gathered, and the souls that they had acquired in Haran, and they set out to go to the land of Canaan. And they came to the land of Canaan.

**6** And Abram passed through the land to the place of Shechem, to the oak of Moreh. Now the Canaanite was then in the land.

**7** And the LORD appeared to Abram and said, "To your offspring I will give this land." So he built there an altar to the LORD, who had appeared to him.

**8** And from there he moved to the hill country on the east of Bethel and pitched his tent, with Bethel on the west and Ai on the east. And he built there an altar to the LORD and called on the name of the LORD.

**9** And Abram journeyed on, going and setting out toward the Negeb.

**10** Now there was a famine in the land. So Abram went down to Egypt to sojourn there, for the famine was severe in the land.

**11** And it came to pass, when he was about to enter Egypt, that he said to Sarai his wife, "Behold, I know that you are a woman beautiful in appearance.

**12** And it will come to pass, when the Egyptians see you, they will say, 'This is his wife.' Then they will kill me, but they will let you live.

**13** Say, I pray, that you are my sister, so that it may go well with me on your account, and that my life may be spared because of you."

**14** And it came to pass, when Abram entered Egypt, that the Egyptians saw that the woman was very beautiful.

**15** And the princes of Pharaoh saw her and praised her to Pharaoh. And the woman was taken into the house of Pharaoh.

**16** And he dealt well with Abram for her sake; and he had sheep, and oxen, and male donkeys, and male servants, and female servants, and female donkeys, and camels.

**17** But the LORD afflicted Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai, the wife of Abram.

**18** And Pharaoh called Abram and said, "What is this you have done to me? Why did you not tell me that she was your wife?

**19** Why did you say, 'She is my sister,' so that I took her for myself as a wife? Now then, here is your wife; take her and go."

**20** And Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him, and they sent him on his way, with his wife and all that he had.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 12 spans Folios 7r–7v.

   **Folio 7r** (GEN_012_page_001.jpg): The transition from Genesis 11 to Genesis 12 is visible on this folio. The closing verses of the Terah narrative (Genesis 11:31–32) appear at the foot of the preceding columns, and Genesis 12 opens in the right-hand column with the divine address to Abram. The Masoretic three-column layout continues. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) appears in verse 1 (the call), verse 7 (the theophany at Shechem), and verse 8 (Abram calling on the name of the LORD). The place-names שְׁכֶם (Shechem), בֵּית־אֵל (Bethel), הָעַי (Ai), and הַנֶּגֶב (the Negeb) are legible in the manuscript. The key phrase לֶךְ לְךָ (lekh-lekha) in verse 1 is clearly visible as the opening imperative of the divine summons. The threefold territorial command in verse 1 — מֵאַרְצְךָ וּמִמּוֹלַדְתְּךָ וּמִבֵּית אָבִיךָ (from your land, from your kindred, from your father's house) — is legible with its characteristic structure of three descending-concentric terms. The fivefold blessing formula of verses 2–3 is clearly visible in the text. The narrative aside וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי אָז בָּאָרֶץ ("now the Canaanite was then in the land") at verse 6 is visible, set within the flow of the travel narrative.

   **Folio 7v** (GEN_012_page_002.jpg): This folio opens with the continuation of the Egypt narrative from approximately verse 14 (the Egyptians seeing Sarai's beauty) through verse 20 (Pharaoh dismissing Abram), and then continues into the opening of Genesis 13. The manuscript shows the return of Abram from Egypt in its characteristic three-column Masoretic layout. Key terms are legible: שָׂרַי (Sarai), פַּרְעֹה (Pharaoh), נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים (great plagues), and the final dismissal formula in verse 20 — וַיְצַוּ עָלָיו פַּרְעֹה (and Pharaoh commanded concerning him). The word שָׁלַח (sent, dismissed) is visible in the final verse.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The chapter heading "CAP. XII" is visible in the left margin of page 8 (GEN_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg). The beginning of Genesis 12 in the LXX is legible, opening with the divine command ἔξελθε ἐκ τῆς γῆς σου ("go out from your land"), which renders lekh-lekha without the emphatic doubling present in the Hebrew. The blessing pericope of verses 2–3 is readable in the three-column uncial script. Page 9 (GEN_012_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the narrative of Abram in Egypt through the end of the chapter, with the wife-sister episode and the plague upon Pharaoh's house legible. The LXX text of the Vaticanus agrees closely with the MT in this chapter, with the key variants noted in the table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. לֶךְ לְךָ — "Go" (v. 1) — The Emphatic Imperative**

The divine command that opens Genesis 12 is built upon two words that are among the most discussed in the entire patriarchal narrative:

לֶךְ לְךָ מֵאַרְצְךָ

The verb לֶךְ (lekh) is the Qal imperative, second-person masculine singular, of the verb הָלַךְ (halakh, "to go, to walk"). The addition of לְךָ (lekha, "for yourself, to yourself") is the reflexive/ethical dative construction — the dative of interest, sometimes called the dativus commodi. It attaches personal investment, urgency, or reflexive emphasis to the verbal action.

The phrase lekh-lekha can be rendered in several ways:

- **"Go for yourself"** — rendering the reflexive dative as grammatically explicit: the journey is for Abram's own sake, his benefit, his destiny. This reading emphasizes the personal stake in the command.
- **"Go!"** or **"Go forth!"** — treating lekha as a purely emphatic intensifier with no separable semantic content: the command simply means "go, emphatically."
- **"Go, yourself"** — treating it as an address that highlights the second-person singular: you, specifically you, go.
- **"Get yourself going"** — a more colloquial rendering that captures the emphatic urgency.

The LXX Vaticanus renders: **ἔξελθε ἐκ τῆς γῆς σου** — "go out from your land." The Greek ἔξελθε (exerchomai imperative) is simply the command to depart, with no attempt to render the emphatic lekha. The LXX thus loses the reflexive emphasis entirely — it translates the territorial extent (out of your land) but not the psychological interior of the command.

The manifest note is precise: the LXX "renders lekh-lekha as ἔξελθε ἐκ, losing the emphatic doubling."

The LOGOS formal translation renders: **"Go from your land."** This decision requires explanation:

The formal equivalence principle requires faithfulness to the source structure. However, "Go for yourself from your land" is grammatically awkward in English and can suggest a meaning ("for your own benefit") that may not be primary. The phrase "Go from your land" preserves the imperative force and its full territorial scope. The threefold structure of the command — from your land, from your kindred, from your father's house — is itself the structural carrier of the emphatic force. The repetition of "from" (מֵ, me) three times creates a triple distancing — Abram must leave everything that defines him — and this structure carries in English the weight that the Hebrew lekha intensifier carries in the Hebrew.

A translation note is the appropriate place to recover what the translation line cannot represent: lekh-lekha carries reflexive urgency. The call is personal and irreversible. The journey that begins in verse 4 ("So Abram went") with the single word וַיֵּלֶךְ (vayelekh — and he went) is the answer to lekh-lekha: the command is fulfilled in its own verb form.

**2. The Threefold Territorial Formula (v. 1) — Concentric Abandonment**

The command of verse 1 lists three things Abram must leave:

1. מֵאַרְצְךָ (me'artsekha) — "from your land"
2. וּמִמּוֹלַדְתְּךָ (umimmoladetkha) — "from your kindred" (literally "from your birthplace/nativity")
3. וּמִבֵּית אָבִיךָ (umibeit avikha) — "from your father's house"

The movement is from large to small, from broad to intimate: land (the national/regional scope), kindred/birthplace (the tribal/clan scope), father's house (the immediate family scope). Yet paradoxically, each item is more specific and more intimate than the last. The land is impersonal; the father's house is the most personal. The command moves inward as it escalates the demand.

The Hebrew word מוֹלֶדֶת (moledet) means "birth, kindred, birthplace, native place." It derives from the root יָלַד (yalad, "to beget, to bear"), which has been the dominant verb of the genealogies throughout Genesis 5, 10, and 11. Now the same root appears in the thing Abram must leave: his moledet — the very matrix of biological and generational identity that the toledot genealogies have been constructing for twelve chapters. Abram is called to leave the moledet precisely as the narratives of moledet reach their culmination. The formal translation renders "kindred" — the standard English equivalent for the clan/birth-kinship sense — in preference to "birthplace," which is also possible but loses the relational aspect.

The LXX renders: ἐκ τῆς γῆς σου καὶ ἐκ τῆς συγγενείας σου καὶ ἐκ τοῦ οἴκου τοῦ πατρός σου — "from your land and from your kindred and from the house of your father." The LXX uses συγγενείας (syngeneias, "kinship, family") for moledet — confirming the relational rather than topographical reading. The formal translation follows suit.

**3. The Fivefold Blessing Formula (vv. 2–3) — Structure and Scope**

The divine speech of verses 2–3 contains one of the most carefully structured passages in the Abrahamic narrative. Grammatically it consists of a series of five divine actions or states, followed by a final climactic statement:

**First person divine actions (vv. 2–3a):**
1. וְאֶעֶשְׂךָ לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל (ve'e'eskha legoy gadol) — "and I will make of you a great nation"
2. וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ (va'avarekekha) — "and I will bless you"
3. וַאֲגַדְּלָה שְׁמֶךָ (va'agaddlah shemekha) — "and I will make your name great"
4. וַאֲבָרֲכָה מְבָרְכֶיךָ (va'avarakha mevarekhekha) — "and I will bless those who bless you"
5. וּמְקַלֶּלְךָ אָאֹר (umekallalkha a'or) — "and him who dishonors you I will curse"

**The purpose clause (v. 2b):**
וֶהְיֵה בְּרָכָה (veheye berakha) — "so that you will be a blessing"

**The universal conclusion (v. 3b):**
וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה (venivrekhu vekha kol mishpechot ha'adamah) — "and in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed"

The formal translation renders each element in sequence, preserving the cumulative structure without collapsing or reordering. The structural key is the progression from individual to universal: the promises begin with one man (Abram) and one name (his), then extend through relational blessing and cursing, and culminate in the blessing of "all the families of the earth."

The word translated "families" is מִשְׁפָּחוֹת (mishpachot), the plural of מִשְׁפָּחָה (mishpachah), which means "family, clan, kind" — specifically the basic kinship unit of Israelite society, larger than the house (בַּיִת) but smaller than the tribe (שֵׁבֶט). The phrase כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה (kol mishpechot ha'adamah, "all the families/clans of the ground/earth") uses אֲדָמָה (adamah, "ground, land, earth") rather than the more neutral אֶרֶץ (eretz) — it is the same word used in Genesis 2 for the ground from which Adam was formed. The choice of adamah rather than eretz may be significant: "all the clans of the ground" evokes human solidarity, the shared creaturely condition of those formed from the dust.

The formal translation renders "all the families of the earth" — using "earth" for adamah as the most natural English equivalent in this context.

**4. וְנִבְרְכוּ — "Shall Be Blessed" (v. 3) — The Niphal Crux**

The final clause of verse 3 contains one of the most exegetically consequential grammatical questions in the entire Hebrew Bible:

וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה

The verb וְנִבְרְכוּ (venivrekhu) is the Niphal third-person plural perfect with waw-consecutive of the verb בָּרַךְ (barakh, "to bless"). The Niphal stem is the passive/reflexive stem in Hebrew — it can bear either a passive meaning ("be blessed") or a reflexive meaning ("bless themselves").

The two main readings:

- **Niphal passive:** "all the families of the earth shall be blessed in you" — the blessing flows through Abram to all peoples; they receive blessing as a divine gift mediated through him. This is the reading adopted by the formal LOGOS translation and by the majority of English versions. It is the reading quoted in the New Testament (Galatians 3:8; Acts 3:25).

- **Niphal reflexive:** "all the families of the earth shall bless themselves by you" — the nations will invoke Abram's name when pronouncing blessings on one another, saying "may you be blessed as Abram was blessed." This is a reflexive idiom of invocation, comparable to the Hithpael at Genesis 22:18 and 26:4, where the verb clearly carries reflexive force (הִתְבָּרֲכוּ, hitbarakhu). The Hithpael is the more emphatic reflexive form; the Niphal here is formally ambiguous.

The Hebrew text at Genesis 12:3 specifically uses the Niphal (וְנִבְרְכוּ), not the Hithpael (as in 22:18). This grammatical distinction is the center of the debate. The Niphal can be either passive or reflexive; the Hithpael is more decisively reflexive. Many scholars argue that the Niphal here should be read reflexively — "bless themselves by you" — on the grounds that: (a) the Hithpael parallel at 22:18 and 26:4 favors a reflexive reading for the root in blessing contexts; (b) the reflexive reading fits the narrative logic of Abram's growing renown; (c) the passive reading may be a later theological development.

The counter-argument for the passive reading: (a) the Niphal frequently carries straightforwardly passive meaning throughout the Hebrew Bible; (b) the quotations in Galatians 3:8 and Acts 3:25 reflect the LXX, which reads ἐνευλογηθήσονται (passive "will be blessed"), suggesting a continuous tradition of passive reading; (c) theologically, the passive reading preserves the initiative of God — the nations do not bless themselves but are blessed — which is consistent with the larger covenantal theology of the Abrahamic narrative.

The LOGOS formal translation adopts the Niphal passive: **"all the families of the earth shall be blessed."** This decision aligns with: (a) the LXX Vaticanus (ἐνευλογηθήσονται, future passive) as read in the manuscript; (b) the grammatical range of the Niphal; (c) the New Testament interpretive tradition; (d) the theological coherence of a blessing that originates with God and is mediated through Abram rather than invoked by the nations through their own initiative. The reflexive reading is documented here as a grammatically live alternative.

**5. שְׁכֶם — Shechem / אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה — The Oak of Moreh (v. 6)**

Abram's first recorded stopping point in Canaan is at Shechem — specifically at the אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה (elon moreh). The word אֵלוֹן (elon) means "oak" or "terebinth" — a large, ancient tree. The word מוֹרֶה (moreh) means "teacher, diviner" (from יָרָה, yarah, "to teach, to show") — the "oak of Moreh" may be a sacred tree associated with divination or instruction, a cultic site. The LXX Vaticanus renders τὴν δρῦν τὴν ὑψηλήν (the tall oak / the high oak), reading מוֹרֶה as an adjective of height (interpreting the root as related to רוּם, to be high) rather than as the participial noun "teacher." The formal translation renders "the oak of Moreh," following the MT's participial reading as the more precise rendering.

The verse closes with a significant narratorial aside:

וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי אָז בָּאָרֶץ

"Now the Canaanite was then in the land."

This brief comment is a remarkable intrusion of the narrator's perspective into the narrative. The phrase "then" (אָז, az) implies temporal contrast: the Canaanite was in the land at that time — implying by contrast that, at the time of the narrator's writing, the Canaanite is no longer (or not exclusively) in the land. This is widely read as a compositional marker suggesting the text reached its present form after the Israelite settlement of Canaan (post-Joshua), when the statement "the Canaanite was then in the land" would carry the force of a historical note rather than a self-evident present condition. The formal translation preserves the phrase in full without comment within the translation text, rendering "Now the Canaanite was then in the land."

**6. וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל אַבְרָם — "And the LORD appeared to Abram" (v. 7) — The First Theophany**

Verse 7 records the first explicit divine appearance (theophany) to Abram:

וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל אַבְרָם וַיֹּאמֶר לְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת

"And the LORD appeared to Abram and said, 'To your offspring I will give this land.'"

The verb וַיֵּרָא (vayyera') is the Niphal wayyiqtol of רָאָה (ra'ah, "to see") — the Niphal is passive: "and he was seen," conventionally rendered "appeared." The construction וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל (the LORD appeared to) is the standard biblical formula for a divine appearance — it is not a description of the mechanism of the theophany but a statement of divine initiative: the LORD made himself seen.

This is the pivotal land-promise: "To your offspring I will give this land." The word זֶרַע (zera', "offspring, seed") is singular in form but collective in meaning — it can refer to one descendant or to a lineage. Here, in context, it refers to Abram's collective posterity. The promise is future ("I will give") and unconditional in this statement — no conditions are attached.

Abram's response is to build an altar: וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה הַנִּרְאֶה אֵלָיו ("so he built there an altar to the LORD, who had appeared to him"). The participial relative clause הַנִּרְאֶה אֵלָיו ("who had appeared to him") identifies the LORD with the theophany — the altar commemorates this specific divine encounter. The formal translation preserves this participial clause.

**7. וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה — "He Called on the Name of the LORD" (v. 8)**

At the second altar-building, at the hill country between Bethel and Ai, the narrative records:

וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה

"And he called on the name of the LORD."

This phrase is the standard biblical expression for worship and invocation. The formula קָרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה (qara' beshem YHWH, "to call on the name of the LORD") denotes:
- Prayer addressed to YHWH specifically, using the divine name
- Proclamation of YHWH's identity (compare Exodus 34:5, where God "proclaims the name of the LORD")
- Cultic invocation at a sacred site

The two altar-building notices (vv. 7 and 8) together establish Abram as a worshiper of YHWH who consecrates the land of Canaan — he marks the territory with altars and with the name of YHWH before he possesses a single acre of it. This is a narrative of faith: Abram worships before the promise is fulfilled.

**8. וַיֵּרֶד אַבְרָם מִצְרַיְמָה — "Abram Went Down to Egypt" (v. 10) — The Wife-Sister Episode**

The famine narrative of verses 10–20 presents one of the most morally complex passages in the patriarchal history. Several translation decisions require comment:

**(a) "To sojourn there" (v. 10):** The verb לָגוּר (lagur, "to sojourn") describes a temporary residence — Abram enters Egypt not as a settler but as a גֵּר (ger, "sojourner, resident alien"), one who lives in a land without citizenship or property rights. The formal translation renders "to sojourn there," preserving the temporary, alien quality of the stay.

**(b) "Beautiful in appearance" (v. 11):** The Hebrew phrase יְפַת מַרְאֶה (yefat mar'eh, "beautiful of appearance") is a standard biblical beauty formula. The word יְפָה (yefah, "beautiful, fair") and מַרְאֶה (mar'eh, "appearance, sight") together describe external physical beauty. The formal translation renders "beautiful in appearance" — the most direct English equivalent.

**(c) "They will kill me, but they will let you live" (v. 12):** Abram's calculation is stark and self-serving: the Egyptians will murder the husband to possess the beautiful wife. The Hebrew is brutally direct: אֹתִי יַהֲרֹגוּ וְאֹתָךְ יְחַיּוּ (oti yahargu ve'otakh yechayyyu, "me they will kill, and you they will let live"). The juxtaposition of the two verbs — הָרַג (harag, "to kill, to murder") and חָיָה Piel (chiyyah, "to keep alive, to preserve life") — is deliberate. The formal translation preserves the stark verbal contrast.

**(d) "I pray" (v. 13):** The particle נָא (na') is an enclitic particle of entreaty or urgency: "please, I pray." Abram's request "say, I pray, that you are my sister" uses this particle — the request is urgent and personal. The formal translation renders "I pray" as the most dignified English equivalent of this entreaty particle.

**(e) The moral ambiguity:** The narrative does not editorialize. Abram's deception is recorded without authorial comment. The narrative outcome — Sarai is taken into Pharaoh's harem (v. 15), Abram is enriched (v. 16), God strikes Pharaoh with plagues (v. 17), and Pharaoh expels Abram with his wife (vv. 18–20) — presents a deeply uncomfortable picture: the patriarch enriches himself through his wife's jeopardy, is corrected not by divine rebuke to Abram but by plague on an innocent party (Pharaoh), and departs wealthier than he arrived. The formal translation renders all of this precisely as the text presents it, without softening, addition, or omission.

**9. נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים — "Great Plagues" (v. 17)**

The word נֶגַע (nega', "plague, affliction, stroke") used in verse 17 is the same root employed in Exodus for the ten plagues upon Egypt (מַכָּה, makkah is also used, but נֶגַע appears in related plague texts). The irony is sharp: God "afflicts Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai" — the structure of the Egypt narrative in Genesis 12 anticipates and prefigures the Exodus. Abram goes to Egypt in a famine; he is enriched; his wife is taken; God afflicts Egypt with plagues; Abram departs with great possessions and with his wife restored. The Exodus pattern — descent to Egypt, affliction, plagues, departure with wealth — is already present in miniature in the story of Abraham. Whether this is intentional typology or retrospective patterning by the final redactor is debated; the formal translation preserves the vocabulary that sustains the connection.

**10. "And Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him, and they sent him on his way" (v. 20)**

The dismissal formula in verse 20 is formal and emphatic: וַיְצַוּ עָלָיו פַּרְעֹה אֲנָשִׁים (Pharaoh commanded men concerning him) — Abram does not leave of his own accord but is escorted out by Pharaoh's agents. The verb שָׁלַח (shalach, "to send, to let go") is used: "they sent him on his way." The same verb שָׁלַח will appear repeatedly in Exodus in the context of Pharaoh "letting go" (שַׁלַּח) the Israelites. The formal translation renders "they sent him on his way" — the most precise English equivalent of the verb and its complement.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 12

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 8–9 (GEN_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_012_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter heading "CAP. XII" is visible in the left margin of page 8. The following variants from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 12:1 | לֶךְ לְךָ ("go — lekh-lekha") | ἔξελθε ἐκ ("go out from") | "Go from your land" | LXX loses the emphatic doubling of lekh-lekha; MT followed |
| 12:2 | וֶהְיֵה בְּרָכָה ("so that you will be a blessing") | καὶ ἔσῃ εὐλογητός ("and you will be blessed") | "so that you will be a blessing" | LXX shifts from active ("be a blessing") to passive ("be blessed"); MT followed |
| 12:3 | וְנִבְרְכוּ (Niphal — passive/reflexive) | ἐνευλογηθήσονται (future passive) | "shall be blessed" | LXX reads as passive; supports LOGOS passive rendering; see extended note |
| 12:6 | אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה ("oak of Moreh") | τὴν δρῦν τὴν ὑψηλήν ("the tall/high oak") | "the oak of Moreh" | LXX reads מוֹרֶה as height adjective; MT participial reading followed |
| 12:8 | וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה ("called on the name of the LORD") | ἐπεκαλέσατο ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματι κυρίου (same) | "called on the name of the LORD" | Agreement; identical rendering |
| 12:10 | כִּי כָבֵד הָרָעָב ("for the famine was severe") | ἴσχυσεν γὰρ ὁ λιμός ("for the famine prevailed") | "for the famine was severe" | Near agreement; MT's כָּבֵד (heavy/severe) vs. LXX's "prevailed" — MT followed |
| 12:16 | וַחֲמֹרִים וּגְמַלִּים ("donkeys and camels") | LXX: ἐναντία αὐτοῦ πρόβατα ("oxen, sheep, etc.") | "male donkeys... and camels" | MT followed; LXX similar but slight variation in animal list order |
| 12:17 | נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים ("great plagues") | πληγαῖς μεγάλαις ("great plagues") | "great plagues" | Full agreement between MT and LXX |
| 12:19 | וָאֶקַּח אֹתָהּ לִי לְאִשָּׁה ("so that I took her for myself as a wife") | ἔλαβον αὐτὴν ἐμαυτῷ γυναῖκα (same) | "I took her for myself as a wife" | Agreement |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Pivot of Genesis — From Primeval History to Patriarchal History**

Genesis 12 is the structural center of the entire book of Genesis, and one of the most significant turning points in the Hebrew Bible. The first eleven chapters of Genesis — the Primeval History — have traced a downward spiral of human failure: the expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3), the first murder (Genesis 4), the flood (Genesis 6–9), the Table of Nations (Genesis 10), and the scattering at Babel (Genesis 11). Each episode presents the problem of human disorder and divine response. But none of the responses has resolved the underlying condition.

Genesis 12:1–3 is God's new answer. Not a flood, not a scattering, not an expulsion — but a call. Out of the dispersed nations, God calls one man. Out of all the families of the earth, one is chosen — not to be exclusive but to be the vehicle through which "all the families of the earth shall be blessed" (v. 3). The particular is called for the sake of the universal. This is the theological architecture of the entire Hebrew Bible in miniature.

**The lekh-lekha Structure of the Call (vv. 1–3) — A Three-Part Promise**

The divine speech of verses 1–3 operates on three levels simultaneously:

1. **Command** (v. 1): Leave everything — land, kindred, father's house — and go to an unnamed land ("that I will show you"). The destination is withheld. Abram must go without knowing where he is going (compare Hebrews 11:8: "he went out, not knowing where he was going").

2. **Promise** (vv. 2–3a): Seven divine gifts are enumerated — a great nation, blessing, great name, being a blessing, blessing for those who bless, curse on those who dishonor, and through him blessing for the nations. The number seven in a list of divine gifts carries deliberate weight in a tradition where seven is the number of completeness.

3. **Universal scope** (v. 3b): The promise breaks out of the individual and national frames to encompass "all the families of the earth." The Abrahamic calling is not tribal — it is cosmological. The election of Abraham is not the exclusion of the nations but the means of their blessing.

**The Itinerary (vv. 4–9) — Abram Marks Canaan with Altars**

The journey itinerary of verses 4–9 is spare and precise:
- Haran → Canaan (v. 5)
- Shechem / oak of Moreh: first altar (v. 7)
- Hill country east of Bethel and west of Ai: second altar (v. 8)
- Toward the Negeb (v. 9)

Abram moves through Canaan from north (Shechem) to south (the Negeb) without settling or purchasing land. He marks the land with altars. The land is promised but not yet possessed. The altars are acts of anticipatory faith — Abram worships the God who gave him a promise of land he does not yet own. The narrative of Genesis 12–25 will be the long story of how the promise given in 12:1–3 is provisionally received, repeatedly threatened, and ultimately confirmed.

**The Egypt Episode (vv. 10–20) — Precursor, Parallel, and Moral Complexity**

The Egypt narrative is one of the most theologically charged passages in the Abrahamic cycle. It has long been recognized as anticipating the Exodus:

| Genesis 12 | Exodus |
|---|---|
| Famine drives Abram to Egypt (v. 10) | Famine drives Jacob's family to Egypt (Gen. 46) |
| Sarai is taken by Pharaoh (v. 15) | Israelites enslaved by Pharaoh |
| The LORD afflicts Pharaoh with plagues (v. 17) | The LORD afflicts Egypt with ten plagues |
| Abram departs with great possessions (v. 20) | Israel departs with "plunder" from Egypt (Exod. 12:36) |

Whether the Genesis 12 account is a prefiguration of the Exodus (intentional typology) or the Exodus tradition has shaped the retelling of the Abram story (retrospective patterning) is one of the perennial debates of Pentateuchal scholarship. The formal translation does not adjudicate this question but preserves the vocabulary — especially נְגָעִים (negaim, "plagues") — that sustains the connection.

**The Moral Ambiguity of Abram's Deception**

The text presents Abram as both the recipient of divine promise and the agent of a morally questionable deception. He instructs his wife to lie — or at least to conceal the full truth — in order to protect himself at her expense. He enriches himself through her jeopardy (v. 16). He is corrected not by YHWH directly but by the plagues upon Pharaoh, and he departs under Pharaoh's reproach rather than divine blessing.

The narrator offers no moral evaluation. There is no word of condemnation of Abram, and no word of divine approval either. Pharaoh's rhetorical questions (vv. 18–19) — "What is this you have done to me? Why did you not tell me that she was your wife?" — function as an implicit moral rebuke: a pagan king voices the moral clarity that the patriarch lacks in this moment. The formal translation preserves this uncomfortable structure without addition, omission, or editorial softening. The silences of the text are as significant as its words.

— LOGOS
